subject: Chemistry
solution:
In a tetrahedral crystal field, the d-orbitals split into a lower energy $e$ set and a higher energy $t_2$ set. The energy of an electron in the $e$ orbital is $-0.6 \Delta_t$ and in the $t_2$ orbital is $+0.4 \Delta_t$. Since $\Delta_t$ is generally small, tetrahedral complexes form high-spin configurations.

The Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) is given by:
$\text{CFSE} = (-0.6 n_e + 0.4 n_{t_2}) \Delta_t$

For $d^2$: The configuration is $e^2 t_2^0$.
$\text{CFSE} = 2 \times (-0.6) + 0 = -1.2 \Delta_t$ (A $\rightarrow$ III)

For $d^4$: The configuration is $e^2 t_2^2$.
$\text{CFSE} = 2 \times (-0.6) + 2 \times (0.4) = -1.2 + 0.8 = -0.4 \Delta_t$ (B $\rightarrow$ IV)

For $d^6$: The configuration is $e^3 t_2^3$.
$\text{CFSE} = 3 \times (-0.6) + 3 \times (0.4) = -1.8 + 1.2 = -0.6 \Delta_t$ (C $\rightarrow$ I)

For $d^8$: The configuration is $e^4 t_2^4$.
$\text{CFSE} = 4 \times (-0.6) + 4 \times (0.4) = -2.4 + 1.6 = -0.8 \Delta_t$ (D $\rightarrow$ II)

Therefore, the correct match is A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The formula of tetraaquadichloridochromium (III) chloride is $[Cr(H_2O)_4Cl_2]Cl$.

In this complex, there is one ionizable chloride ion present outside the coordination sphere.

The dissociation reaction is:

$[Cr(H_2O)_4Cl_2]Cl \rightarrow [Cr(H_2O)_4Cl_2]^+ + Cl^-$

Number of moles of the complex $= M \times V = 0.05 \times \dfrac{100}{1000} = 0.005$ moles.

Since one mole of the complex yields one mole of chloride ions, the number of moles of $Cl^-$ ions produced is $0.005$ moles.

When excess $AgNO_3$ is added, the chloride ions precipitate as $AgCl$:

$Ag^+ + Cl^- \rightarrow AgCl \downarrow$

Number of moles of $AgCl$ precipitated $= 0.005 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ moles.

The value is $5$.

Answer: $5$

The color of <math><msup><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is violet as it absorbs the yellow light.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
In an octahedral complex, the d-orbitals split into $t_{2g}$ and $e_g$ levels.

For $d^3$: Configuration is $t_{2g}^3 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons = $3$.

For $d^4$ (low spin): Configuration is $t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons = $2$.

For $d^5$ (high spin): Configuration is $t_{2g}^3 e_g^2$. Number of unpaired electrons = $5$.

For $d^6$ (high spin): Configuration is $t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$. Number of unpaired electrons = $4$.

For $d^7$ (low spin): Configuration is $t_{2g}^6 e_g^1$. Number of unpaired electrons = $1$.

Total number of unpaired electrons = $3 + 2 + 5 + 4 + 1 = 15$.

Answer: $15$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Some ligands are able to produce strong fields in which case, the splitting will be large whereas others produce weak fields and consequently result in a small splitting of d orbitals.

In general, ligands can be arranged in a series called spectrochemical series according to which maximum splitting is done by the strongest ligand which is cyanide (CN-) ion in this case.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$[Ni(CO)_4]$:
Ni is zero-valent with configuration $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^2$.
CO is a strong field ligand causing complete pairing in the filled $3d^{10}$ orbital.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[NiCl_4]^{2-}$:
Ni is $+2$ with configuration $[Ar]3d^8$.
Cl is a weak field ligand producing high-spin tetrahedral geometry with configuration $3d^8$: five orbitals with two electrons paired, three electrons unpaired.
Unpaired electrons = 2.

$[PtCl_2(NH_3)_2]$:
Pt is $+2$ with configuration $[Xe]4f^{14}5d^8$.
Pt(II) adopts square planar geometry with strong-field splitting leading to complete pairing: $5d^8$ configuration places all electrons in bonding orbitals.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$:
Ni is $+2$ with $3d^8$ configuration.
CN is a strong field ligand.
Square planar geometry is preferred, resulting in all electrons paired.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[Pt(CN)_4]^{2-}$:
Pt is $+2$ with $5d^8$ configuration.
CN is a strong field ligand producing square planar geometry with complete pairing.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

Total unpaired electrons = 0 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2.

solution:
The colors of wavelengths are absorbed in decreasing order of wavelengths.

λ red > λ yellow > λ green > λ blue

as ΔE=hcλ

Δ E Red < Δ E Yellow < Δ E Green < Δ E Blue

L1 < L3 < L2 < L4

As the order of ligand field strength provides the order of relative crystal field splitting energies.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The absorption of visible light and hence coloured nature of the transition metal cation is due to the promotion of one or more unpaired - $d$ - electron from a lower to higher level within same $d$-subshell. Hence higher will be the number of unpaired electron higher will be the absorpion in visible light.
The electronic configuration of the given elements is $\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}(18)=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^0 4 s^0-$ no unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$.
$\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}(18)=1 s^2 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^0 4 s^0-$ no unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$. $\mathrm{V}^{3+}(20)=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^2 4 s^0$ - Two unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$. $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(28)=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^{10} 4 s^0-$ no unpaired $\mathrm{e}^{-}$. hence $\left[\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ will most likely absorb visible light.

subject: Chemistry
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
Crystal field theory explains d-d transitions in complexes based on ligand field strength.
Statement I: Ligand field strength order is I⁻ < Br⁻ < Cl⁻ < F⁻. Since Br⁻ is weaker than Cl⁻, [CoBr₄]²⁻ has smaller crystal field splitting (Δ) than [CoCl₄]²⁻. The d-d absorption energy equals the splitting energy, so [CoBr₄]²⁻ absorbs lower energy light. TRUE
Statement II: I⁻ is a much weaker field ligand than Cl⁻. Therefore, [CoI₄]²⁻ has smaller crystal field splitting than [CoCl₄]²⁻, not larger. The energy separation in [CoI₄]²⁻ is less than in [CoCl₄]²⁻. FALSE
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.

△t= 49 △o

△t < △o

CoCl2+6H2O⟶CoH2O6Cl2 

CoH2O62+ →Conc. HCl [CoCl4]2-

Hence, X=CoH2O62+, Y=CoCl42-, Z=Tetrahedral

(Red light has highest wavelength of 700 nm while blue is the shortest with a wavelength of about 480 nm)

So, ∆0 (strength of ligands) order  will be L2>L1>L3.

(As ∆0 α 1λabs )

Hence, order will be L2>L1>L3.

Therefore, Strength of ligand ∝∆Absorption.

solution:
Cr(CN)63- ion, oxidation state of Cr is +3 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d3. There are 3 unpaired electrons in 3d orbital.  

(A). Cr(CN)63-

No. of unpaired electrons =3

 

FeH2O62+ ion, oxidation state of Fe is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d6. There are 4 unpaired electrons in 3d orbital.  So, you can say the hybridisation here would be sp3d2.

(B). FeH2O62+

No. of unpaired electrons =4

CoNH363+ in this oxidation state of central metal atom is +3 and it has no unpaired electrons.
(C). CoNH363+

No. of unpaired electrons =0

NiNH362+ the oxidation state of Ni is Ni2+ and it has two unpaired electrons.
(D) NiNH362+

No. of unpaired electrons =2
So the correct option among the given is A.
